title: Skill integrity hash verification (post-audit tamper detection)
description: The current skill security audit runs once at import time. A skill that passes the audit could later be modified — by a compromised dependency, a malicious write to ~/Sandbox/, or a supply chain attack on a skill fetched from an external source. Add SHA-256 hash storage at audit time and verify the hash on every skill load/invocation, blocking execution if the hash doesn't match.
rationale: The reading note's core insight is that the XZ Utils attack was undetectable by any single-point inspection — the threat was cumulative, incremental modification over time, after trust was established. Mira's audit is exactly this single-point inspection: it catches malicious code at import, but not post-import tampering. The note says 'social trust cannot be formally verified' — for Mira, the analog is that a one-time audit cannot verify ongoing integrity. Hash verification converts the trust model from 'trusted at time T' to 'verified at every invocation', closing the temporal gap the XZ attack exploited.
diff_description: In soul_manager.py: (1) In audit_skill(), after a skill passes, compute sha256 of the skill file's contents and write {skill_name: hash} to soul/skill_hashes.json. (2) Add a new function load_skill(skill_name) that reads the skill file, computes its current sha256, compares against the stored hash in skill_hashes.json, and raises SecurityError (logging the mismatch) if they differ. (3) Any call site that currently loads a skill directly should go through load_skill(). For skills with no stored hash (legacy skills loaded before this change), treat as unverified and re-run the full audit before executing, then store the hash.
